text: By the end of this section, you'll understand why most physical hazards in a bakery are maintenance failures, why pre-op is the early warning system that catches them, and why the metal detector is your last line of defense, not your first. Physical hazards in a bread bakery are almost always the result of equipment wear or mechanical failure. But the sanitation manager is typically the first person to notice, because pre-op puts you in front of equipment before production starts, looking closely at surfaces and components. Let's walk through the most common sources, missing bolts and nuts. This is the most common physical hazard in bakeries with vibrating equipment, and bread bakeries have plenty. Tweety mixers vibrate during mixing. Doe dividers vibrate as they portion. Conveyors vibrate as product moves. Pan stackers vibrate as they align. Over time, vibration loosens fasteners until they fall off. A missing bolt means two things. First, there's a foreign body somewhere in your process. A steel bolt in a dough batch is a serious safety issue. Second, the parts that bolt held together are now metal to metal without proper securing, generating fragments. One missing bolt, two types of contamination. During pre-op, check equipment guards, mixer fittings, conveyor joints, divider assemblies, and pan stacker mechanisms for missing or loose fasteners. If something feels different from yesterday, investigate. Missing fastener protocols should require a documented search. If you can't find the bolt, assess whether it could have entered the product stream. Metal fragments from worn conveyor belts. Metal mesh and modular belts with metal hinge pins wear over time, generating fine particles and eventually larger fragments as links or pins break. Belt wear accelerates with dough containing seeds or grains. Inspect belts regularly for stretched links, worn pins, cracked modules, and fraying edges. Lubricant drips from oven chains and bearings. Your oven's chain system and the bearings on conveyor rollers, molders, and dividers all require lubrication. When seals wear, lubricant migrates onto surfaces that can contact product. Every lubricant used where product contact is possible must be food grade H1 rated. If non-food grade lubricant contaminates product, that's a chemical hazard with regulatory consequences. During pre-op, check under oven conveyor chains, below bearings on overhead equipment, and under any lubrication point. An oily film or shiny residue means investigation is needed. Flaking paint and degraded surfaces. In an aging bakery, painted surfaces above production areas flake. Ceiling panels degrade. Rust develops on overhead steelwork. Each generates contamination that falls onto product surfaces. During pre-op, look up. Check ceilings, overhead structures, and upper walls for peeling paint, rust, and watermarks. Broken equipment guards and covers. Perspex guards on slicers, metal covers on dividers, and plastic shields on conveyors can crack or chip. Clear plastic fragments from a broken slicer guard are particularly dangerous because they're difficult to detect visually and with a metal detector. Now the metal detector. Most bakeries have one at the end of the line, after bagging and before dispatch. It's designed to detect and reject products containing metal above a certain size, typically two millimeters for ferrous metal. But it can't detect plastic, glass, stone, or wood. It has sensitivity limits, and it relies on calibration, testing, and reject verification. If you're relying on the metal detector to catch physical hazards, your upstream controls have already failed. Your primary controls are pre-op inspection, preventive maintenance, fastener management, and belt replacement schedules. The metal detector is the last chance, not the first line. Recap. Physical hazards are predominantly maintenance failures, missing bolts from vibration, metal fragments from worn belts, lubricant drips from failing seals, flaking paint, and broken guards. Pre-op is the early warning system. The metal detector is your last defense. If you're relying on it, everything upstream has failed.
